text: CHING-NING-CHU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kwei-chu, division of Ganshun-fu.
CHING-NING-PU, a town of China, in the prov. CHI of Chih-le, in N lat. 40° 59′ 45", and long. W of Peking 0° 44′ 12″.
CHINGOMA, an island on the Mozambique coast of South Africa, at the mouth and formed by two branches of the Zambeze. It is about 75 m. in length, and 30 m. in breadth.
CHING-PING, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Ho-nan, division of Nan-gan-fu.
CHING-PU-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Hu-nan, division of Paou-king-fu.
CHING-PUNG, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kwang-tung, division of Kea-ying-chu.

CHING-TIH-FU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, on the outside of the great wall. The division comprises 6 districts, and was incorporated with Chih-le in 1778. It is celebrated for its medicinal herbs. - The town, called also Geho or Gehol, is situated on the r. bank of the Ge-ho, 120 m. NE of Peking. It is of considerable extent, and contains an imperial residence. A little to the N are the temples of Phou-tho-tsoung-ching-miao and Siu-mi-fou-cheou-miao, two of the most remarkable of the sacred edifices which abound in this locality.

CHING-TING-FU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Chih-le.—The division comprises 14 districts.—The town is situated on the l. bank of the Hueto, in N lat. 38°, and long. E of Peking 1° 43′ 30″. It is about 4 m. in circumf.; and contains several monuments, erected in honour of heroes, in the number of whom is included the first emperor of the dynasty of Han. The adjacent mountains abound with medicinal plants.

CHING-TU-FU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Setchuen. The division comprises 15 districts.—The town, situated in N lat. 30° 41′ 40″, and long. 12° 18' W of Peking, is large and populous, and enjoys an active commerce. It is intersected by several canals, which connect it with the Yang-tszekiang, and which are crossed by numerous bridges. This town was formerly the imperial residence, and one of the finest and most important cities in the empire. It suffered greatly during the civil wars of 1646.
CHING-WU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shan-tung, division of Tsaou-chu-fu.
CHING-YANG, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Ho-nan, division of Ju-ming-fu.
CHIN-HAE-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Che-kiang, division of Nang-po-fu, 9 m. NE of Ning-po, on the Tong-hai or Eastern sea.
CHIN-HAI, a town on the S coast of the peninsula of Corea, at the mouth of the Han-kiang, in N lat. 34° 40'.
CHIN-HEUNG-CHU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Yun-nan, division of Tungchuen-fu.
CHINI, a large village of Northern Hindostan, in the district of Kunawar, at an elevation on the Himalaya range of 10,200 ft. above sea-level. Grapes grow in great luxuriance in the environs.
CHINI, or CHIRI, a small island of Costa-Rica, in the gulf of Nicoya, in N lat. 10°.

CHINIAN (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, arrond. of Saint- Pons. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,711; in 1841, 9,230. The town is 12 m. ESE of Saint-Pons, on the Bernazober. Pop. in 1841, 3,627. It contains manufactories of cloth and hosiery—the former on an extensive scale for the Levant tradeand tanneries.
CHININI, a large village of Hindostan, in the Punjab, on the Taui river, which has its sources in the mountains to the E of it, and falls into the Chinab about 50 m. lower down.
CHIN-KEW, a district and town of China, in the <sup>Q</sup>C prov. of Ho-nan, 40 m. S by E of Tching-tchou, on an affluent of the Kwo-ho.
CHIN-LEW, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Ho-nan, division and 12 m. SE of Kaefung-fu.

CHIN-LI, a town of China, on the N coast of the island of Hainan, 12 m. W by S of Kiong-tchou. CHINLEY-BUGSWORTH AND BROWNSIDE, a chapelry in the p. of Glossop, Derbyshire, 31 m. NW of Chapel-in-le-Frith. Pop. 996.
CHIN-MOU, a town of China, in the prov. of Shen-si, on the Kin-ye-ho river, 50 m. NE of Yu-lin.
CHIN-NAN-CHU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Yun-nan, division and 21 m. NW of Tsu-heung-fu.
CHINNAPUTTEN, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, 40 m. NE of Seringapatam. In 1800 it consisted of about 1,000 houses; and in the vicinity is a fort substantially built of stone.

CHINNARA'N, a town of Persia, in Khorassan, 43 m. NW of Meshid. It was the stronghold of the celebrated Mammush Khan, who held it against the army of Ahmed Shah Abdalli, king of Kabul, for seven months. It is now nearly in ruins. Its walls have been dilapidated, and, when Fraser visited the place in 1821, enclosed only about 300 wretched hovels. The chummun, a meadow of C., a fine tract of natural pasture, extends from the Chushmahe- Gilass, a lake in which one of the head-streams of the Meshid river rises, to Radkán, over a tract 40 m. in length, and from 7 to 8 m. broad.
CHINNOCK (East), a parish of Somerset, 43 m. SW of Yeovil. Area 2,090 acres. Pop. 735.
CHINNOCK (MIDDLE), a parish of Somerset, 32 m. NE of Crewkerne, on the Parret. Area 280 acres. Pop. 222.
CHINNOCK (West), a parish of Somerset. Area 600 acres. Pop. 561.
CHINNOR, a parish of Oxfordshire, 5½ m. SE of Thame. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 1,308.
CHINNUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Deccan, in the prov. of Bider, on the N bank of the Godavery, 10 m. above the confluence of the Pranhuta, and 140 m. NE of Hydrabad.

CHINON, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et- Loire. The arrond. comprises an area of 166,133 hect., including the 7 cant. of Azay-le-Rideau, Bourgueil, Chinon, L'Isle-Bouchard, Langeais, Sainte- Maure, and Richelieu. Pop. in 1831, 88,898; in 1846, 91,244.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 17,449; in 1841, 17,239.—The town occupies a picturesque situation on the r. bank of the Vienne, 30 m. WSW of Tours. Pop. in 1789, 4,446; in 1821, 6,548; in 1831, 6,959; and in 1846, 6,785. It contains a communal college, and has some manufactories of woollen fabrics. The trade consists chiefly in grain, wine, and dried fruit, the preparation of the latter of which forms an important branch of local industry. Fairs are held five times a-year.—This town was formerly fortified, and sustained several sieges. It was taken by the English under Henry II., who died here, and still contains the castle in which Charles resided, when he gave audience to Joan of Arc, which, though in ruins, as 'the Windsor of the French' is carefully preserved from further dilapidation.
CHINRAIAN-DROOG, a town and hill-fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, 56 m. NNW of Bangalore, and 93 m. NNE of Seringapatam.

CHINRAIPATAM, or Chin-Raya-Patam, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, 40 m. NW of Seringapatam, on an affluent of the Hennavutti. It is defended by a stone fort, surrounded by a ditch, and contains about 900 houses. CHI
CHIN-SI, a town of China, in the prov. of Sechuen, 50 m. SW of Ya, on an affluent of the Yang-Kiang.

CHINSURA, or CHINSURAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, on the W bank of the Hugli, 3 m. above Chandernagore, and 20 m. N of Calcutta. It is defended by a good fort. The houses, generally two stories in height, are substantially built of brick. Pop. 14,000. It contains several schools for the education of the native children.—In 1656, permission being granted by the sultan Mohammed Choudjah, a factory was established here by the Dutch. It was subsequently fortified, and after some years became a town of great commercial importance. In 1795 it was taken by the English; in 1815 was restored by treaty to the Dutch; but it was subsequently transferred to the British government.
CHINTACHE, a lake of California, in the valley of San Joachin. It is a feeder, in common with the Tula lakes, of the San Joachin river, and has an alt. of at least 1,000 ft. above sea-level.
CHIN-VY-TAO, an island in the Yellow sea, off the W coast of Corea, in N lat. 39° 20'.

CHINY, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, 9 m. SSW of Neufchâteau, on the l. bank of the Samoy. Pop. in 1836, 1,063. It was formerly a town of considerable importance, but has suffered much from the wars of which it has been the theatre. It has some trade in wood and charcoal, and in the environs are iron-works.
CHIN-YANG-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kiang-su, division of Taetsang-chu.
CHIN-YUEN-CHU, or CHIN-HWAN-CHU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Yunnan. It comprises only the district of Gan-lo-heen.

CHIN-YUEN-FU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Kwei-chu. The prov. comprises Chin-Yuen-heën and 5 other districts. The cap. is situated in N lat. 27° 1′ 12", and long. 8° 10' W of Peking, 120 m. ENE of Kwei-chu. It is irregularly built, and contains no edifices worthy of notice. Oranges and pomegranates grow in great abundance in the surrounding territory.

CHIOGGIA, or CHIOZZA, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the gov., delegation, and 17 m. S of Venice, on the island or Lido di Palestrina, to the N of the embouchure of the Brenta, and near the Porto-di-Chioggia, which forms one of the five entrances of the lagunes of Venice. Pop., with the suburb Sottomarina, in 1837, 25,357. It is built on piles, and contains a cathedral, several churches, an hospital, a theological seminary, a gymnasium, an orphan's asylum, a theatre, and numerous convents. It possesses several building-docks, and manufactories of cordage, and in the environs are extensive salt-works. The port is good, and the adjacent fisheries important. Fairs are held twice a-year. This town is noted as the scene of several engagements between the Venetians and Genevese. The Porto-di-Chioggia is one of the deepest of the entrances to the lagunes of Venice, and, next to that of Malamocca, the most frequented. It is defended by the two forts of Coroman and San Felice, and by several batteries.
CHIONI, a gulf on the W coast of the island of Corsica, in N lat. 42° 10'. It is about 21 m. in depth, and 1 m. in average breadth. A small river of the same name flows into its upper part.
CHIOS. See Scio.
CHIPANIR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, principality and 55 m. SSW of Bhopal, on the N bank of the Nerbudda.
CHIPICAN LAKE, a small lake of Upper Canada, in the township of Sarnia, 2 m. N of Port Sarnia, and separated from Lake Huron by a ridge of high land.

CHIPIONA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 19 m. N of Cadiz, partido of Santa Maria, on the l. side of the estuary of the Guadalquivir, 3 m. NE of San-Lucar-de-Barrameda.—Also a town 3 m. SW of San-Lucar-di-Barrameda, on the Atlantic, at the mouth of the Guadalquivir. Pop. 1,731. It contains a parish church and a convent, and considerable remains of Roman and other ancient edifices. Fishing forms the chief branch of local industry.
CHIPKIEU BALKAN, a pass in the Balkan range, between Adrianople and Rutshuk, by Ternova. It is steep, but there is no part of it over which artillery could not be conveyed.
CHIPOLA, a town of Jackson co., in the state of Florida, U. S.
CHIPOUNSKOI (CAPE), a headland of Russia in Asia, on the SE coast of Kamtschatka, in N lat. 53°.

CHIPPENHAM, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 41 m. NNE of Newmarket. Area 4,500 acres. Pop. 666.—Also a parish, borough, and market-town of Wilts, 93 m. W by road of London, 13 m. NE of Bath, on the S bank of the Avon, on the great road from London to Bath. Area of p. 9,100 acres. Pop. of p. 5,438.—The town is pleasantly situated on the Avon, by which it is nearly surrounded, and over which is a stone-bridge of 22 arches, which constitutes a fine feature in the approach from the N road. The streets, which have outgrown the old borough, are lighted with gas. There were, some years ago, a number of woollen manufactories here; but this branch of trade is now extinct. There are a few grist mills and tanneries. The town is a great thoroughfare on the Bath and Bristol roads, and in the line of the Wilts and Berks canal, and of the Great Western railway, which has a station here 933 m. from London. As its name denotes, it has long been celebrated for its markets. C. is a borough by charter as well as by the reform act. The several parishes of C., Hardenhuish, and Langley-Burrel, and the extra-parochial space called Pewisham, formerly the forest of C., are included within the borough by the new boundaries act; the new borough thus extending far beyond the old in every direction. Pop. of the new and extended borough, in 1831, 5,270; in 1841, 6,606. Number of electors registered in 1836, 239; in 1847, 307. C. is a place of great antiquity, and under the West Saxon kings, was one of the first cities in the island. "In those times," says Camden, "it was the country-house of the Saxon kings." There are in the vicinity of the town two chalybeate springs, formerly celebrated, but now seldom visited by the rich and fashionable.
CHIPPERFIELD, a hamlet in the p. of Kings- Langley, Hertfordshire. Pop. 569.

CHIPPEWA, a large village of Upper Canada, partly in the townships of Willoughby and Stamford, and intersected by the Chippewa or Welland river, an affluent of the Niagara. It is 2 m. from the great falls. Pop. 1,000. It derives its chief importance from the facilities of its situation for ship building. It has regular steam communication during the season with Buffalo; and is on the important line of stages from Buffalo to Detroit.—Also a county of tonshire, 6 m. NNE of Banbury. Area 2,440 acres. the state of Michigan, U.S., comprising an area of Pop. 545. 2,000 sq. m., bounded on the N by Lake Superior, and on the E by St. Mary's river. Pop. in 1840, 534.—Also a river in the state of Wisconsin, U. S., which rises on the confines of the state of Michigan, runs S and receives the tribute of several lakes, and Red Cedar river; and after a total course of 150 m., enters the Mississippi below Lake Pepin.-Also a London to Oxford, and consists principally of one CHI township of Wayne co., in the state of Ohio, 18 m. NE of Wooster, and 113 m. NE of Columbus. It is generally fertile and well cultivated. Pop. 1,787. The village is called also Doylestown.
CHIPPEWAY, a town of Beaver co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 7 m. NW of Beaver. The surface is hilly, and the soil a fertile loam. Pop. in 1840, 610.

CHIPPEWAYAN, or CHIPEWYAN (FORT), a fort of British North America, in the district of Athabasca, on a rocky point on the NW bank of the lake of the same name, sometimes called the Lake of the Hills, at the confluence of Slave river, in N lat. 58° 43', and W long. 111° 18'; 240 m. from the Medize portage. It is the centre of the commercial operations of the North-West company. On the S bank of the lake is Old C.—The mean temp. here in 1825-26, was 31° 59'; the mean of lowest temp. 6° 59'; of highest, 51° 83'.

CHIPPEWAYANS, or CHIPEWYANS, a family of North American Indians located on the Chippeway river in Michigan; in Canada on the Ottawa; and in the NW territories, between the Athabasca and Great Slave lakes, and Churchill river. Franklin describes the C. of the latter district as having broad faces, projecting cheek-bones, and wide nostrils, but generally good teeth and fine eyes. Those of Michigan are described as tall and thin, well-formed and active, and as distinguished from the Missouri Indians by the absence of the aquiline nose. They subsist principally on fish, wild rice, and deer. From the nature of the country which they inhabit, they are distributed into families rather than into tribes. Between them and the Sioux a long and deadly feud has existed. Carver says, that of the four classes into which the principal languages of the natives of North America may be divided, the language of the C. appears to be the most prevailing: "it being held in such esteem, that the chiefs of every tribe dwelling about the Great lakes, or to the W of these, on the banks of the Mississippi, with those as far S as the Ohio, and as far N as Hudson's Bay, consisting of 30 different tribes, speak this language alone in their councils, notwithstanding each has a peculiar one of his own. It will probably in time become universal among all the Indian nations, as none of them attempt to make excursions to any great distance, or are considered as qualified to carry on any negotiation with a distant band, unless they have acquired the C. tongue. At present, besides the C., to whom it is natural, the Ottawas, the Sankies, the Ottagaumies, the Killistinoes, the Mipagons, the bands about Lake Le-Pleuve, and the remains of the Algonkins, or Gens-de-Terre, all converse in it with some little variation of dialect; but whether it be natural to these nations or acquired, I was not able to discover. The C. tongue is not encumbered with any unnecessary tones or accents, neither are there in it any words that are superfluous; it is also easy to pronounce, and much more copious than any other Indian language."
CHIPPICOTON, a town of the North West Territory, U. S., on the W bank of Lake Michigan, 65 m. N of Chicago.
CHIPPING, a parish and township of Lancashire, 71 m. E of Garstang, on a branch of the Hodder. Area of p. 8,990 acres. Pop. 1,675. Area of township 6,010 acres. Pop. 1,168.
CHIPPING-BARNET. See BARNET-CHIPPING.
CHIPPINGHURST, a hamlet in the p. of Cuddesden, Oxfordshire. Area 140 acres. Pop. 18.
CHIPPING-LAMBOURN, a township in the p. of Lambourn, Berks. Pop. 1,333.
CHIPPING-NORTON, a parish and township of Oxfordshire. Area of p. 4,780 acres. Pop. 3,031. Area of township 3,430 acres. Pop. 2,629.
CHIPPING-ONGAR, a parish of Essex. Area 480 acres. Pop. 870.
CHIPPING-SODBURY, a parish of Gloucestershire. Area 120 acres. Pop. 1,273.
CHIPPING-WARDEN, a parish of Northamp-

CHIPPING-WYCOMBE, or HIGH WYCOMBE, a parish, borough, and market-town, in the hund. of Desborough, co. of Buckingham; 29 m. W by N of London. Area of p. 6,380 acres. Pop. in 1801, 4,248; in 1831, 6,299; in 1841, 6,480.—The town is situated in a deep narrow valley on the road from broad street, with some smaller ones branching off at | right angles. The houses are in general well-built. The church is an ancient structure, in the early style of English architecture, with a square embattled tower, crowned with modern pinnacles. The interior is handsome, and consists of a nave, aisles, and chancel. A rivulet, called W. stream, flows through the town, and afterwards falls into the Thames a little below Marlow. The adjoining country is rich and fertile, agreeably diversified with hills covered with wood. Wycombe-abbey, the seat of Lord Carington, is situated in a bottom near the town. The manufacture of paper is carried on here to a great extent. Lace is manufactured, and some articles of wood, such as chairs. The prosperity of the town, however, has been chiefly sustained by the great traffic constantly passing through it, a source of emolument not likely to have been benefited by the opening of the Great Western railway, which passes about 9 or 10 m. to the S of the town. The borough returns two members. The number of electors registered, in 1837, was 446; in 1847, 346. The old parliamentary boundaries were coincident with those of the municipal borough; the new boundaries are coincident with those of the parish.—This place is undoubtedly of great antiquity. The word cheaping, is the Saxon term for 'a market,' and proves it to have been occupied by that people. The ancient remains called Desborough-castle are generally supposed to have been of Saxon origin, and to have been intended to keep off the invasions of the Danes. Prince Rupert made a successful attack on the parliamentary forces quartered here shortly after the battle of Reading. W. gives the titles of earl and baron to the marquess of Lansdowne.
CHIPSTABLE, a parish of Somerset, 1 m. SW of Wiveliscombe. Area 2,420 acres. Pop. 389.
CHIPSTEAD, a parish of Surrey, 24 m. NE of Gatton, near the Surrey railway. Area 2,490 acres. Pop. 666.
CHIPULA PEAK, a summit of Northern Hindostan, in the prov. of Kumaon, 50 m. NE of Almora, in N lat. 29° 56', E long. 80° 24'. It rises to the height of 13,257 ft. above sea-level.

CHIPURANA, a river of Peru, in the department of Truxillo, which descends from the NE side of the E branch of the Peruvian Andes; forms a circuit to the NW; and unites with the Huallaga, 115 m. S of the confluence of that river with the Maranon.
CHIQUEIRRO-DO-ALLEMAO, a small town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, district of Ouro-Preto.

CHIQUIACOBA (LAKE), an alpine lake in Peru, in the plain of Bonbon, at an alt. of 13,200 ft. above sea-level. It is the source of the Rio-Huanuco or Huallaga; and is separated only by a low ridge of hills from the Laguna-de-Quiluacocha, in which the Rio-Mantaro, a principal branch of the Apurimac, rises.

CHIQUIAN, a large village of Peru, in the district of Caxatambo. The pop. is composed of Creoles, Indians, and Mestizoes, whose principal occupation is farming and grazing. Fine ponchos of wool and cotton are manufactured here.
CHIQUILIGASTA, a town of La Plata, in the prov. and 25 m. S of Tucuman, on an affluent of the Tala.

CHIQUIMULA, a district of Guatimala, bounded by the Atlantic on the N, and Honduras on the E. The productions of the soil are maize, rice, cocoa, melons, cotton, and sugar. Cattle, hogs, horses, and mules, are extensively reared. The pop. is above 80,000. The vernacular language is the Chorti.— The table-land of Guatimala is united to that of Honduras by a belt of low land sometimes called the CHI Isthmus of C. Measured across this isthmus, the two oceans are about 150 m. apart.
CHIQUIMULA-DE-LA-SIERRA, a town of Guatimala, in the district of Chiquimala, and 75 m. ENE of Guatimala, in N lat. 14° 20', on an affluent of the Motagua. The pop. is estimated at upwards of 3,000.
CHIQUITA MAR, a salt lake in the pampas of Buenos Ayres, forming one of a series of marshy lakes through which the Salado passes in the upper part of its course.

CHIQUITOS, a province of Bolivia, in the depart. of Santo-Cruz-de-la-Sierra, bounded on the NE and E by the Sierra Agoapehy and S. Fernando or Doirados, by which it is divided from the Brazilian prov. of Matto-Grosso; on the S by the Gran Chaco; on the W by the provinces of Charcas and Cochabamba, from which it is separated by the Parapiti, Chiquitos, and San Miguel, Branco, or Magdalena; and on the N by the territory of the Moxos. It comprises a territory about 285 m. in length from E to W, and 270 m. in breadth from N to S. In the N it is intersected by several ramifications of the Sierra Guarayos; in the interior is an extensive chain of lagunes. The climate, which is warm and humid, is rendered extremely insalubrious by the immense forests by which the greater part of the prov. is covered.—The Ubahy or Magdalena river, in that part of its course which lies between Lake Ubahy and the parallel of 16° S, is sometimes called the Rio-C.
CHIQUITOS (SAN-JOSE-DE), a small town, the capital of the above prov., situated in S lat. 17° 50', 170 m. E by N of Santa-Cruz-de-la-Sierra.
CHIRAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Lozère, cant. of Saint-Germain-du-Thil, on the r. bank of the Rioulone. Pop. 1,238.
CHIRALA, a town of New Grenada, in the district and 20 m. SE of Socorro, on an affluent of the Galinazo.
CHIRAN, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, district of Sarun, 13 m. ESE of Chupra, and near the N bank of the Ganges.
CHIRASSIMONT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Symphorien-de-Lay. Pop. 2,062.
CHIRAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Chabanais. Pop. 1,042.
CHIRBURY, a parish and village in Salop, 3 m. NE of Montgomery. Area of p. 11,880 acres. Pop. 1,591.—The v. is agreeably situated in a fertile vale on the borders of Wales.
CHIRCARI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, 16 m. ENE of Jeitpore.
CHIRCOTE, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Cohahuila, 60 m. SE of Chihuahua.
CHIRDON, a township in the p. of Gaystead in Northumberland, 6 m. from Bellingham. Pop. 60.

CHIRE, a river of Mozambique, flowing into the Zambezi, on the l. bank, 20 m. above its mouth.— Also a river of New Grenada, descending from the Sierra-Chita, and flowing, in an E course, to the Meta, which it joins on the l. bank, a little above the junction of the Casanare.—Also a town of New Grenada, 40 m. WSW of the town of Casanare, on the l. bank of the C. river.
CHIRENS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Voiron. Pop. 2,009.
CHIRGONG, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bundlecund, 10 m. ENE of Jansi.
CHIRICO-NUOVO (SAN), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, in the district and 15 m. E of Potenza. Pop. 1,500.
CHIRICO-RAPARO (San), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, district and 20 m. ENE of Lagonegro. Pop. 3,747.

CHIRIQUI LAGOON, the E part of a great bay of the gulf of Mexico, on the N coast of Costarica. It is about 26 m. long, and in some places 12 or 13 m. wide. Its principal entrance, called the Valencia or Valientes channel, is about 2 m. wide, with from 14 to 23 fath. water. It contains several islands; and, in N lat. 9° 16', W long. 82° 10', is connected with Almirante bay, forming the W part of the great bay, by various shallow arms and creeks. Several streams descending from the Cabecares mountains, flow into it, of which the principal are the C. river, and the Chrico-Mola.

CHIRK, a parish and village in Denbighshire, 8 m. from Wrexham. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,099; in 1841, 1,611. The v. is finely situated on the brow of a hill. The Ellesmere canal is carried over the Dee, and the picturesque vale of Ceriog, in this p., by a magnificent aqueduct 710 ft. in length, and 22 ft. wide, with 10 arches of 40 ft. span each. Extensive coal and lime works are wrought in the vicinity of C .- Within a mile of the v. stands C. castle, founded in the 11th cent., and still the seat of the Myddletons. It is a massive quadrangular pile 160 ft. long, by 100 ft. wide, with massive bastion towers at each angle.
CHIRLITZ, or CHIRLICE, a village of Moravia, in the circle and 3 m. ESE of Brunn, on the l. bank of the Schwarzawa. Pop. 603.
CHIRMEN. See KHIRMEN.
CHIRNSIDE, a parish and village in Berwickshire, on the Whitadder stream. Area of p. about 5,000 acres. Pop. 1,203. The v. is 9 m. NW of Berwick, and 6 m. E of Dunse.

CHIRRA, a station and convalescent depot on the Kasiah hills in Upper India, in N lat. 25° 12½, E long. 91° 35′, 30 m. SE of Sylhet. It consists of a tract of table-land about 2 m. long, and 1 m. broad, at an alt. of 4,000 ft. above sea-level, and lying on a stratum of grey sandstone. The climate from November till April is delicious; but the falls are heavy, and the fogs thick, during the rainy season. Rice, Indian corn, yams, potatoes, cabbages, brocoli, and turnips, are cultivated here; and fruit is good and abundant.
CHIRRA-PUNJI, a Kasiah village, 1 m. N of the above station, and at an elevation of 200 or 300 ft. above it.
CHIRONICO, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, district of Levantina, 20 m. NNW of Bellinzona, at an alt. of 2,646 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 887.

CHIRRIPO', a volcanic mountain of Central America, in the Oriental department of the state of Costarica, in N lat. 9° 47', W long. 83° 46'.—A river of the same name descends from its NE flanks, and flows N to the Matina, which it joins on the r. bank above the confluence of the Barvilla. It is navigable for boats above its point of confluence, and frequently inundates the surrounding country.
CHIRTA, a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Assuay, prov. and 110 m. E of Jaen, on the r. bank of the Altamayo or Imassa.
CHIRTON, a township in the p. of Tynemouth, Northumberland, 1 m. WSW of North Shields. Pop. 4,360.
CHIRU, an islet of New Grenada, in the bay of Panama, off the headland of the same name which forms the N side of the gulf of Parita.
CHIRU. See CHERU.
CHIRVAN. See SHIRVAN.

CHIRVINTY (Bolchoi), a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 30 m. NW of Vilna; on the 1. bank of a river of the same name about <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> of a mile to the W, is the town of Chirvinty (Maloï).

CHISAPANI, or Chisaghurri, a fortress of Hindostan, in the Nepal territory, subdivision of Nepal-Proper, 15 m. SW of Khatmandu. It occu- II CHI pies an important situation at the entrance of the Nepal valley, and has been considerably strengthened and enlarged by the British government; but its site is commanded by the adjacent heights.
CHISELHAMPTON, a parish of Oxfordshire, 71 m. SE of Oxford. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 153.
CHISELHURST, a parish of Kent, 11 m. SE of London. Area 4,050 acres. Pop. 1,792.
CHISENBURY, a tything in the p. of Enford, Wilts. Pop. 149.
CHISHALL (GREAT), a parish of Essex, 8 m. WNW of Saffron-Waldon. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 466.
CHISHALL (LITTLE), a parish of Essex, 8 m. W of Saffron-Waldon. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 96.
CHIS-KAR, or Tsitsikar, a town of Mantchuria, in N lat. 47° 24', and long. E of Peking 7° 27' 48", on the l. bank of the Nonni, and 250 m. SW of Sagalin-Oola.
CHISLEBOROUGH, a parish of Somerset, 4 m. NNE of Crewkerne, bounded on the W by the Parret. Area 810 acres. Pop. 540.
CHISLEDON, a parish of Wilts, 31 m. SE of Swindon. Area 5,710 acres. Pop. 1,176.
CHISLETT, a parish of Kent, 7 m. NE of Canterbury, on a branch of the Stour. Area 5,180 acres. Pop. 1,097.
CHISONE. See CLUSONE.
CHISSAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Montrichard. Pop. 1,028. It produces good wine.
CHISSEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Saint-Gengous-le-Royal. Pop. 1,014.
CHISSEY - EN - MORVANT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Lucenay, 13 m. N of Autun. Pop. 1,395.

CHISSOUEMATOU, a river of Lower Canada, which takes its rise on the high lands to the S of Mistassimie lake, and running NW, enters the Assuapmoussoin near the lake in which that river has its source.
CHISWELL'S ISLANDS, a group of islands near the S coast of Russian America, to the NW of Montague island, in N lat. 59° 34'. They appear to be completely destitute of vegetation.
CHISWICK, a parish of Middlesex, 7 m. WSW of St. Paul's cathedral, on the N bank of the Thames. Area 1,120 acres. Pop. 5,811.
CHISWORTH, a township in the p. of Glossop, Derbyshire. Pop. 532.

CHITA, a town of New Grenada, in the district and 80 m. WSW of Casanare, and to the E of the mountain range of the same name. Pop. 900. The surrounding country is fertile, and contains extensive salt-mines. The mountain forms a section of the long chain which divides the basin of the Magdalena on the W, from that of the Meta on the E.
CHITAPUR, or CHETAPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 55 m. NNW of Lucknow, on the r. bank of the Jamnooaree.
CHI-TCHOU-OUE, or Shin-Show, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Hu-pi, division and 165 m. W of King-chu-fu, on an affluent of the Yang-tse-kiang.
CHI-TCHU, a district and town of China, in the S part of the prov. of Sze-chuen, noted as the birthplace of the emperor Yu, founder of the dynasty of Yya.
CHITENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Contres. Pop. 1,189.
CHITHURST, a parish of Sussex, 31 m. WNW of Midhurst. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 232.

CHITI, or CITI, a village of the island of Cyprus, on the S coast, on the Tesio, which flows into the 2 M Mediterranean, 5 m. W of Cape C., and 30 m. SSE of Nicosia. It is the Citium of the ancients, the birth-place of Zeno. The cape is a low flat point, in N lat. 34° 49′ 55′, and E long. 33° 38′ 15″.

CHITLONG, a small town of Northern Hindostan, in the prov. of Nepal, 15 m. WSW of Khatmandu, in a valley named Lahong Nepal. In 1790 it consisted of a few houses, substantially built of brick and tiled, and two or three stories in height.
CHITO, a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Assuay, prov. and 50 m. NW of Jaen de Bracamoros, near the l. bank of the Chinchupe.
CHITORE. See CHITUR.
CHITOUNG, a town of Burmah, in the kingdom of Pegu, on the E side of a river of the same name, which is here about a mile in breadth.
CHITOWAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 65 m. NNE of Delhi, and 30 m. S of Seharaunpur.

CHITRAL, a district and town of Kunduz, in Kafiristan. The district is a long valley, 100 m. in length, and from 15 to 20 m. in breadth, extending along the S slope of the Hindu-kush, between the parallels of 35° 50' and 36° 30'. The town is on the 1. bank of the Kama or Kuner, 50 m. SE of Badakshan. Pop. 3,000.
CHITRAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre, cant. of Saint-Gaultier. Pop. 376.
CHITRY-LES-MINES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. and 2 m. W of Corbigny, on the r. bank of the Yonne. Pop. 614. It contains a productive silver-mine.
CHITTAE, an island of the Indian ocean, in the Lakadive group, in N lat. 11° 45′, and E long. 73° 15′.

CHITTAGONG, or CHATI-GANG, a district of Hindostan, in the SE extremity of Bengal; between the parallels of 21° and 23° N; bounded by Tippurah on the N; the Birman empire on the E; the ceded district of Arracan on the S; and the bay of Bengal on the W. It is 120 m. in length, by 25 m. in average breadth; and has an area of 2,987 sq. m. The islands of Mascal, Hattia, Sundip, and Bamini, are annexed to this district. The interior is mountainous, and covered with jungle; but between the ranges of hills are numerous plains and valleys susceptible of rich cultivation, and capable of producing coffee, pepper and spices. The surface along the coast is low and flat, and intersected by numerous streams, flowing on the one side into the gulf of Bengal; and on the other, into the Irawaddi. Of these the Nauf or Naf, flowing into the gulf of Bengal, forming the SE boundary of the district, is not navigable above a few miles from the coast, and intersects a rugged district covered with jungle, and presenting only a few isolated spots of cultivation. The Kamaphuli, or C. river, which rises in Ava, and flowing SW, enters C. through the Mugh range, is the principal stream. This river enters the gulf of Bengal, in about 22° 15' N lat., and at its mouth forms a secure harbour. The town of C., or Islam-abad, is about 11 m. from its mouth, on the W or r. bank of the river, in N lat. 22° 22′, E long. 91° 42′. It was a great emporium in the time of Acbar; and is still the principal port of the district. About 300 vessels, of from 40 to 100 tons, are owned in the place. The river is here 1 m. broad; but all around is jungle spread over undulating surfaces as far as the eye can reach. The houses of the European residents are built upon small detached hills or abrupt mounds, some of them nearly 100 ft. high, which appear to have been formed by water scooping out the channels betwixt them. The native pop. live along the valleys, among plantain, olive, mango, orange, and almond trees, with neat gardens of esculents. The town has a pop. of 12,000, CHI 546 streets are kept in good order, and the language, mode of building, and general aspect of everything, are Bengalee. Salt and rice are the staple commodities of the place, which, until the Birman war, was the farthest SE station of the Company, though there were detachments further down the coast .- About 20 m. to the N of C. is the Sita-cund or Seeta-khoond, a remarkable mineral spring which emits inflammable vapour.—The climate of this district generally resembles that of Bengal; but the rains set in earlier; and the dews at night not unfrequently come down like rains.—The pop. of C. was estimated at 1,200,000 in 1801. This seems an overestimate; at present it probably does not exceed 800,000. A considerable proportion are Mughs from Arracan, a muscular hardy race, who have emigrated into the British territory as husbandmen, pedlars, or mechanics. Among the hill-tribes of C. are the Cukies, and the Chumias. The former are a muscular race, of low stature, with broad, flat, Tartarlike faces. They have none of the Hindu distinctions of caste, and use animal food freely. The latter are of migratory habit, and are more pacific and industrious than the Cukies. The land is held by a great number of small proprietors; but above two-thirds of the surface is still waste.—The army under command of General Morrison assembled at C. with the view of taking possession of Arracan by a route across the chain of hills which form the W boundary of the valley of the Irawaddi; commenced their march in the month of January, 1825; and, after a long and fatiguing march of 150 m. over bad roads, took possession of the town of Arracan on 1st April. A new road has since been formed, which presents a clear route for the march of troops from C. to the Myu river, where there is a good ferry to the island of Akyab.—The district of C. was held by the rajah of Arracan at the beginning of the 17th cent. It was seized by the subahdar of Bengal towards the end of that cent., and in 1760 was ceded to the English. On the acquisition of the Arracan province in 1826, it was politically annexed to it.
CHITTAPETT, a small town and fort of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 78 m. SW of Madras, in N lat. 12° 56′.
CHITTAVAIL, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 40 m. SE of Cuddapah, on an affluent of the Pennair.

CHITTAVADU, a village and fort in the Carnatic, near the base of the Ragery hills, 30 m. NNW of Madras. It was once a considerable place, and the country around is diversified with low wooded hills, tanks, and rice-fields.

CHITTENANGO, a village in Madison co., in the state of New York, U. S., 120 m. WNW of Albany, near the line of the Syracuse and Utica railroad, and on a small stream flowing N into Oneida lake, after a rapid course of 30 m. Pop. 1,000. It is connected by a side cut with the Erie canal.— There is a fine waterfall, forming a cascade nearly 140 ft. in height, on the C. stream.

CHITTENDEN, a county of the state of Vermont, U. S., comprising an area of 500 sq. m.; bounded on the W by Lake Champlain, and intersected by Onion, Lamoille, and Laplotte rivers. It is hilly in the E. and possesses a variety of soil from a light sand to a rich alluvium. Pop. in 1830, 21,765; in 1840, 22,977. - Cap., Burlington. - Also a township of Rutland co., in the same state, 12 m. NE of Rutland, and 40 m. SW of Montpelier. It lies on the Green mountains, between the Connecticut river and Lake Champlain, and is watered by the Philadelphia and White rivers. Pop. in 1840, 644. CHITTLEDRUG, CHITELDRUG, OF CHATRAKAL, and immediately adjacent are several villages. The a subdivision of the principality of Mysore, in Hindostan. It is an open district, with a rich black soil, | but in great part uncultivated. Its capital, of the same name, in N lat. 14° 13', E long. 76° 25', is a town of about 4,000 inhabitants; but was formerly of more importance. It has a very strong fort, built upon a steep mountain. Six gateways in succession, one above the other, lead from rock to rock, through an intricate maze of granite ramparts, to the summit, which commands a bird's eye view of the whole defences. An English garrison is constantly stationed here.
CHITTLEHAMPTON, a parish of Devon, 5½ m. W of South Molton. Area 5,700 acres. Pop. 1,893.
CHITTOE, a tything in the p. of Bishop's Cannings, 5 m. NW of Devizes. Area 880 acres. Pop. 207.
